symbols: papal tiara or triple crown — the supreme sovereignty of God over heaven, earth, and the underworld
raised right hand in blessing — the divine bestowal of grace and the Trinitarian benediction
crystal scepter in left hand — the kingly authority and universal dominion of God over all creation
rich red robes — the blood of Christ, divine love, and the royal majesty of God
jeweled brooch at the breast — the breastplate of the High Priest, suggesting Christ's priestly and kingly offices united
golden inscription above — the Word of God proclaimed over and around the divine presence
ornate throne — the throne of God described in Revelation, from which all judgment and mercy proceed
description: A tall, narrow altarpiece panel showing a majestic frontal figure seated on an elaborate throne. The figure wears a magnificent tiered papal tiara adorned with jewels and a rich crimson-red robe with an ornate jeweled clasp at the chest. The right hand is raised in a gesture of blessing with two fingers extended. The left hand rests on the knee holding a clear crystal or glass scepter. The figure has a full brown beard and a serene, authoritative expression. Behind the head is a large golden halo or arch.
theological_description: Van Eyck's depiction of God Almighty from the Ghent Altarpiece is one of the most theologically rich images in Western art. The figure, sometimes interpreted as God the Father and sometimes as Christ in His divine glory, embodies the concept of the Pantocrator — the All-Ruler who governs the cosmos from His heavenly throne. The triple tiara signifies supreme authority over all realms of existence, echoing the divine titles in Revelation: 'King of kings and Lord of lords' (Revelation 19:16). The blessing gesture with two fingers raised alludes to the two natures of Christ (divine and human) united in one person, while the scepter fulfills the messianic prophecy of Genesis 49:10, that the scepter shall not depart from Judah. The crimson robes recall both the royal dignity of God and the blood of the Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:9). The Latin inscriptions surrounding the figure declare divine attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and eternal being. This panel sits at the apex of the entire Ghent Altarpiece program, above the Lamb of God on the lower register, creating a vertical axis from sacrificial atonement below to glorified sovereignty above — the full arc of redemption made visible.

scripture_refs.reason: The vision of One seated on the throne in heaven, surrounded by glory — the direct theological source for the enthroned divine figure
King of kings and Lord of lords — the supreme sovereign title expressed in the figure's regal vestments and crown
The scepter shall not depart from Judah — the crystal scepter fulfills the messianic prophecy of eternal kingly rule
The Ancient of Days taking His seat, His clothing white as snow — Daniel's throne vision as a precursor to this imagery
We have seen His glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father — the visible manifestation of divine glory
The radiance of God's glory and the exact imprint of His nature, upholding all things — the theological basis for depicting divine majesty
